content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Monophthongs}} is defined as: Spanish vowels are short, crisp, and do not reduce to a schwa in unstressed positions.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Monophthongs' es: Spanish vowels are short, crisp, and do not reduce to a schwa in unstressed positions.
content.usage:
Rule: Monophthongs
Description: Spanish vowels are short, crisp, and do not reduce to a schwa in unstressed positions.

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs}} is defined as: Created by combining a "strong" vowel (/a, e, o/) with a "weak" vowel (/i, u/), e.g., *aire* /ajɾe/, *huevo* /weβo/.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Diphthongs' es: Created by combining a "strong" vowel (/a, e, o/) with a "weak" vowel (/i, u/), e.g., *aire* /ajɾe/, *huevo* /weβo/.
content.usage:
Rule: Diphthongs
Description: Created by combining a "strong" vowel (/a, e, o/) with a "weak" vowel (/i, u/), e.g., *aire* /ajɾe/, *huevo* /weβo/.

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Hiatus}} is defined as: When two strong vowels meet, they form separate syllables (e.g., *teatro* /te.a.tɾo/).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Hiatus' es: When two strong vowels meet, they form separate syllables (e.g., *teatro* /te.a.tɾo/).
content.usage:
Rule: Hiatus
Description: When two strong vowels meet, they form separate syllables (e.g., *teatro* /te.a.tɾo/).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Lenition of /b, d, ɡ/}} is defined as: Between vowels, these plosives become approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *cada* [ˈkaða]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Lenition of /b, d, ɡ/' es: Between vowels, these plosives become approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *cada* [ˈkaða]).
content.usage:
Rule: Lenition of /b, d, ɡ/
Description: Between vowels, these plosives become approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *cada* [ˈkaða]).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Rhotics}} is defined as: Distinction between the alveolar tap /ɾ/ (*pero*) and the alveolar trill /r/ (*perro*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'The Rhotics' es: Distinction between the alveolar tap /ɾ/ (*pero*) and the alveolar trill /r/ (*perro*).
content.usage:
Rule: The Rhotics
Description: Distinction between the alveolar tap /ɾ/ (*pero*) and the alveolar trill /r/ (*perro*).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Yeísmo}} is defined as: In most modern dialects, /ʎ/ (ll) and /j/ (y) have merged into /j/ or [ʝ].
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Yeísmo' es: In most modern dialects, /ʎ/ (ll) and /j/ (y) have merged into /j/ or [ʝ].
content.usage:
Rule: Yeísmo
Description: In most modern dialects, /ʎ/ (ll) and /j/ (y) have merged into /j/ or [ʝ].

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Seseo vs. Distinción}} is defined as: - **Seseo** (Latin America/Canary Is.): 'z' and 'ce/ci' are pronounced as /s/.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Seseo vs. Distinción' es: - **Seseo** (Latin America/Canary Is.): 'z' and 'ce/ci' are pronounced as /s/.
content.usage:
Rule: Seseo vs. Distinción
Description: - **Seseo** (Latin America/Canary Is.): 'z' and 'ce/ci' are pronounced as /s/.

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Syllable-timed Rhythm}} is defined as: Every syllable takes roughly the same amount of time to pronounce, creating a machine-gun rhythm.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Syllable-timed Rhythm' es: Every syllable takes roughly the same amount of time to pronounce, creating a machine-gun rhythm.
content.usage:
Rule: Syllable-timed Rhythm
Description: Every syllable takes roughly the same amount of time to pronounce, creating a machine-gun rhythm.

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasal & Lateral Assimilation}} is defined as: Nasal /n/ and lateral /l/ change their place of articulation to match the following consonant (e.g., *un beso* -> [um ˈbeso], *un gato* -> [uŋ ˈgato], *el coche* -> [eʎ ˈkotʃe]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Nasal & Lateral Assimilation' es: Nasal /n/ and lateral /l/ change their place of articulation to match the following consonant (e.g., *un beso* -> [um ˈbeso], *un gato* -> [uŋ ˈgato], *el coche* -> [eʎ ˈkotʃe]).
content.usage:
Rule: Nasal & Lateral Assimilation
Description: Nasal /n/ and lateral /l/ change their place of articulation to match the following consonant (e.g., *un beso* -> [um ˈbeso], *un gato* -> [uŋ ˈgato], *el coche* -> [eʎ ˈkotʃe]).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sinalefa (Vowel Blending)}} is defined as: When one word ends in a vowel and the next begins with a vowel, they merge into a single syllable (e.g., *mi amigo* -> [mjaˈmiɣo], *su auto* -> [ˈswaw.to], *esta agua* -> [esˈta.ɣwa]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Sinalefa (Vowel Blending)' es: When one word ends in a vowel and the next begins with a vowel, they merge into a single syllable (e.g., *mi amigo* -> [mjaˈmiɣo], *su auto* -> [ˈswaw.to], *esta agua* -> [esˈta.ɣwa]).
content.usage:
Rule: Sinalefa (Vowel Blending)
Description: When one word ends in a vowel and the next begins with a vowel, they merge into a single syllable (e.g., *mi amigo* -> [mjaˈmiɣo], *su auto* -> [ˈswaw.to], *esta agua* -> [esˈta.ɣwa]).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::S-Aspiration & Dropping}} is defined as: In many rapid dialects (Caribbean, Andalusian, Rioplatense), syllable-final /s/ is aspirated to an [h] or omitted entirely (e.g., *los amigos* -> [loh aˈmiɣoh] or [lo aˈmiɣo], *está* -> [ehˈta] or [eˈta]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'S-Aspiration & Dropping' es: In many rapid dialects (Caribbean, Andalusian, Rioplatense), syllable-final /s/ is aspirated to an [h] or omitted entirely (e.g., *los amigos* -> [loh aˈmiɣoh] or [lo aˈmiɣo], *está* -> [ehˈta] or [eˈta]).
content.usage:
Rule: S-Aspiration & Dropping
Description: In many rapid dialects (Caribbean, Andalusian, Rioplatense), syllable-final /s/ is aspirated to an [h] or omitted entirely (e.g., *los amigos* -> [loh aˈmiɣoh] or [lo aˈmiɣo], *está* -> [ehˈta] or [eˈta]).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::D-Omission (Elisión de -d-)}} is defined as: - Final **-d** is often dropped in speech (e.g., *verdad* -> [βeɾˈða], *salud* -> [saˈlu]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'D-Omission (Elisión de -d-)' es: - Final **-d** is often dropped in speech (e.g., *verdad* -> [βeɾˈða], *salud* -> [saˈlu]).
content.usage:
Rule: D-Omission (Elisión de -d-)
Description: - Final **-d** is often dropped in speech (e.g., *verdad* -> [βeɾˈða], *salud* -> [saˈlu]).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Intervocalic -d-** in the ending}} is defined as: ado** is almost always omitted in casual conversation (e.g., *hablado* -> "hablao" [aˈβla.o], *cansado* -> "cansao" [kanˈsa.o]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Intervocalic -d-** in the ending' es: ado** is almost always omitted in casual conversation (e.g., *hablado* -> "hablao" [aˈβla.o], *cansado* -> "cansao" [kanˈsa.o]).
content.usage:
Rule: Intervocalic -d-** in the ending
Description: ado** is almost always omitted in casual conversation (e.g., *hablado* -> "hablao" [aˈβla.o], *cansado* -> "cansao" [kanˈsa.o]).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::B, D, G Lenition}} is defined as: Between vowels or after non-nasals, these consonants soften significantly into approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *nada* [ˈnaða], *lobo* [ˈloβo], *lago* [ˈlaɣo]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'B, D, G Lenition' es: Between vowels or after non-nasals, these consonants soften significantly into approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *nada* [ˈnaða], *lobo* [ˈloβo], *lago* [ˈlaɣo]).
content.usage:
Rule: B, D, G Lenition
Description: Between vowels or after non-nasals, these consonants soften significantly into approximants [β, ð, ɣ] (e.g., *nada* [ˈnaða], *lobo* [ˈloβo], *lago* [ˈlaɣo]).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Agudas}} is defined as: Words ending in a consonant (except 'n' or 's') are stressed on the last syllable (*hablar*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Agudas' es: Words ending in a consonant (except 'n' or 's') are stressed on the last syllable (*hablar*).
content.usage:
Rule: Agudas
Description: Words ending in a consonant (except 'n' or 's') are stressed on the last syllable (*hablar*).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Llanas}} is defined as: Words ending in a vowel, 'n', or 's' are stressed on the penultimate syllable (*casa*, *hablan*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Llanas' es: Words ending in a vowel, 'n', or 's' are stressed on the penultimate syllable (*casa*, *hablan*).
content.usage:
Rule: Llanas
Description: Words ending in a vowel, 'n', or 's' are stressed on the penultimate syllable (*casa*, *hablan*).

content.text: In Spanish phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Esdrújulas}} is defined as: Always have a written accent mark and are stressed on the antepenultimate syllable (*pájaro*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Spanish.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Spanish, la regla de 'Esdrújulas' es: Always have a written accent mark and are stressed on the antepenultimate syllable (*pájaro*).
content.usage:
Rule: Esdrújulas
Description: Always have a written accent mark and are stressed on the antepenultimate syllable (*pájaro*).

content.text: What is the Spanish pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Sinalefa Pro-Tip}}? Spanish speaker speed comes from "Sinalefa". Never pause between words if one ends in a vowel and the next starts with a vowel. Run them together as one sound!
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Spanish.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Spanish para 'Sinalefa Pro-Tip'? Spanish speaker speed comes from "Sinalefa". Never pause between words if one ends in a vowel and the next starts with a vowel. Run them together as one sound!
content.usage:
Tip: Sinalefa Pro-Tip
Spanish speaker speed comes from "Sinalefa". Never pause between words if one ends in a vowel and the next starts with a vowel. Run them together as one sound!

content.text: What is the Spanish pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Cansao" Trick}}? To sound like a native in informal conversation, drop the 'd' in words ending in '-ado'. Say "pesao" instead of "pesado" and "terminao" instead of "terminado".
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Spanish.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Spanish para 'The "Cansao" Trick'? To sound like a native in informal conversation, drop the 'd' in words ending in '-ado'. Say "pesao" instead of "pesado" and "terminao" instead of "terminado".
content.usage:
Tip: The "Cansao" Trick
To sound like a native in informal conversation, drop the 'd' in words ending in '-ado'. Say "pesao" instead of "pesado" and "terminao" instead of "terminado".

content.text: What is the Spanish pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? Keep your vowels "pure." Unlike English, Spanish vowels never turn into a "lazy" sound like the schwa.
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Spanish.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Spanish para 'consejo'? Keep your vowels "pure." Unlike English, Spanish vowels never turn into a "lazy" sound like the schwa.
content.usage:
Tip: Tip
Keep your vowels "pure." Unlike English, Spanish vowels never turn into a "lazy" sound like the schwa.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Mi hijo va de una vez a la escuela."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Mijo va duna vez a la escuela.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Sinalefa
Contiguous vowels 'i-hi' and 'de-u' merge into single syllables in fast speech, eliminating word boundaries.
content.spanish: Spanish: My son goes to school at once.
content.usage: Formal: Mi hijo va de una vez a la escuela. → Connected: Mijo va duna vez a la escuela.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Los mismos muchachos están listos."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Lo' mi'mo' muchachos ehta' liht-o'.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Aspiración de S
Syllable-final 's' is aspirated to a soft breath [h] or dropped entirely in fast conversational dialects.
content.spanish: Spanish: The same boys are ready.
content.usage: Formal: Los mismos muchachos están listos. → Connected: Lo' mi'mo' muchachos ehta' liht-o'.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Está cansado de trabajar para nada."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Está cansao de trabajar pa' na'.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Relajación de D
The dental consonant /d/ between vowels is extremely relaxed, resulting in complete deletion in colloquial fast speech.
content.spanish: Spanish: He is tired of working for nothing.
content.usage: Formal: Está cansado de trabajar para nada. → Connected: Está cansao de trabajar pa' na'.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Dame un beso en mi mejilla."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Dame um beso em mi mejilla.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Asimilación N-M
The nasal /n/ shifts its place of articulation to bilabial [m] before the upcoming bilabial consonants /b/ and /m/.
content.spanish: Spanish: Give me a kiss on my cheek.
content.usage: Formal: Dame un beso en mi mejilla. → Connected: Dame um beso em mi mejilla.
